prompt: Why did you consider a federal power to regulate interstate commerce essential, and what problems were you trying to solve?
ground_truth_signal: states were erecting trade barriers against each other; commercial warfare between states; no uniform regulation caused chaos; Virginia and Maryland disputes over Potomac navigation; Annapolis Convention grew from commerce disputes; national market required national regulation
response:
The regulation of commerce among the several states was among the most pressing defects of our original confederation, and I confess it was a principal object of my concern in the years leading to the Philadelphia Convention.

Under the Articles of Confederation, we witnessed a most pernicious spectacle: the states behaving toward one another as rival nations rather than members of a common union. Virginia would levy duties upon goods from Maryland; New York would tax the commerce of New Jersey and Connecticut as though they were foreign powers; Pennsylvania would retaliate with restrictions of her own. Each state, pursuing what it conceived to be its separate interest, inflicted injury upon its neighbors and ultimately upon itself.

The consequences were manifold and ruinous. First, this commercial warfare bred animosity among states that ought to have been bound together by ties of mutual benefit. I observed with alarm how quickly economic disputes could escalate into political hostilities that threatened the very bonds of union. Second, the absence of uniform commercial regulations created a maze of conflicting rules that impeded the natural flow of trade and prevented the formation of that extended market which would benefit all. A merchant in Richmond could not know what duties or restrictions he might encounter when his goods crossed into North Carolina or Maryland.

Third, and perhaps most dangerous, this commercial chaos invited foreign interference. When the states could not speak with one voice in matters of trade, European powers found it convenient to negotiate separate arrangements with individual states, playing them against one another to secure advantageous terms. This reduced us from a nation to a collection of petty provinces, each scrambling for the favor of powers that had lately been our enemies.

Experience had taught us that mere recommendations from Congress possessed no force when they conflicted with what the states perceived as their immediate interests. The power to regulate interstate commerce could not be left to voluntary compliance or mutual agreement, for such arrangements inevitably collapsed when one party found them inconvenient.

I believed then, and I believe now, that the power to regulate commerce among the states flows naturally from the necessity of preventing the states from injuring one another through discriminatory laws. It is a power defensive in its character rather than aggressive—designed not to aggrandize the federal government but to preserve that harmony among the states without which no union can long endure.

The framers of the Constitution understood that commerce, like war and diplomacy, requires a single directing authority. Just as we could not permit each state to conduct its own foreign policy or maintain its own army, we could not permit each state to regulate commerce according to its own narrow conception of advantage. The result would be not thirteen policies but thirteen conflicts, destructive of the general welfare and ultimately of the particular interests the states sought to protect.
